title: Endometrial Cancer
oneLiner: Most common gynaecological malignancy in developed countries; typically postmenopausal; presents with abnormal uterine bleeding; usually diagnosed early.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Massive acute vaginal bleed with haemorrhagic shock** – rare but life-threatening. Key finding: **haemodynamic instability** (tachycardia, hypotension, pallor) with visible heavy bleeding. Rule out by assessing pulse, BP, and perineal pad count.
- **Pulmonary embolism (PE) from cancer-associated hypercoagulability** – can present with sudden dyspnoea, pleuritic chest pain, hypoxia. Key finding: **unexplained hypoxia (SpO₂ <94% on air) + tachycardia** with no other obvious cause. Urgent CT pulmonary angiogram if high Wells’ score.
- **Uterine perforation or sepsis following endometrial biopsy** – very rare but consider if severe pelvic pain, fever, or peritonism within hours of procedure. Key finding: **rebound tenderness / guarding** post-biopsy.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Postmenopausal bleeding (PMB) – any bleeding ≥12 months after menopause (the “classic” symptom)
  - Perimenopausal / intermenstrual bleeding (in younger women)
  - Thin, blood-stained discharge (serosanguinous)
  - Pelvic pain or pressure (advanced disease)
  - Abdominal distension (advanced: ascites, mass)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Speculum: visible bleeding from os; atrophic or friable cervix; polypoid mass protruding through cervix
  - Bimanual: enlarged, irregular, or fixed uterus; adnexal mass (if ovarian extension)
  - General: pallor (anaemia), lymphadenopathy (inguinal or supraclavicular), hepatomegaly (metastatic)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Benign endometrial atrophy** – PMB with thin endometrium on ultrasound (<4mm), but still needs biopsy to exclude cancer
- **Endometrial polyp / submucosal fibroid** – sometimes indistinguishable; biopsy/hysteroscopy required
- **Cervical cancer** – visible lesion on speculum, watery/offensive discharge; differentiate by cervix appearance + cytology
- **Vulvar / vaginal cancer** – visible lesion, different site
- **Uterine sarcomas** – faster growth, often larger, may have “fleshy” tissue passed; requires histology
- **Hormone-related anovulatory bleeding (perimenopausal)** – irregular cycles, no PMB; endometrial thickness variable
- **Splitter:** If bleeding is truly postmenopausal (no periods >1 year), **endometrial cancer is the diagnosis until proven otherwise** — do not assume benign atrophy without biopsy.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Full set of observations (pulse, BP, SpO₂, NEWS2)
  - Urine pregnancy test (always, to exclude pregnancy-related causes in premenopausal)
  - Speculum examination + bimanual (as above)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (look for anaemia from chronic blood loss)
  - Renal function / liver function (pre-surgery baseline, also look for obstructive jaundice)
  - Coagulation screen (if heavy bleeding or planned biopsy)
  - Ca125 (elevation suggests advanced stage or serous histology; not diagnostic)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Transvaginal ultrasound (TVUSS) – measures endometrial thickness (ET). Cut-off: >4mm in postmenopausal women (NICE) warrants further assessment. Visualise endometrium: regular vs irregular, myometrial invasion? Adnexal masses?
  - **Second-line:** MRI pelvis (for local staging) – assess depth of myometrial invasion, cervical involvement, lymph nodes
  - CT chest/abdomen/pelvis (if high risk or suspicious of metastases)
- **Special tests:**
  - Endometrial biopsy (Pipelle) in clinic – gold standard for histology. If Pipelle fails or bleeding too heavy, hysteroscopy + targeted biopsy under GA.
  - Histology: look for type (endometrioid vs serous/clear cell) + grade + oestrogen receptor status
- **Diagnostic criteria:** No single scoring system. Diagnosis is histopathological confirmation from biopsy or hysterectomy specimen.
- **Never-miss test:** **Endometrial biopsy** – omission is a serious error. A woman with PMB and ET >4mm must have tissue diagnosis before being discharged. If Pipelle yields insufficient tissue, escalate.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If haemodynamically unstable from heavy bleeding: IV access, cross-match, fluid resuscitation, consider tranexamic acid 1g IV (even though not first-line for cancer bleeding, may reduce loss). Inform gynae reg immediately.
  - If suspected PE: high-flow oxygen, check D-dimer (if low clinical probability) or arrange urgent CT pulmonary angiogram. Start LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 1.5mg/kg once daily) if PE confirmed pending anticoagulation.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Admit under gynae oncology team.
  - Arrange endometrial biopsy if not yet done (inpatient if unable to do as outpatient).
  - Staging MRI pelvis (usually done as outpatient, but if inpatient: order if high suspicion).
  - Start VTE prophylaxis (LMWH) unless bleeding contraindicates – all cancer patients at high risk.
  - Check FBC – arrange transfusion if Hb <80 (or symptomatic).
  - Refer to gynae oncology clinical nurse specialist for support and counselling.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Surgery (standard for early stage):** total hysterectomy + bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy ± pelvic lymphadenectomy. Minimally invasive approach preferred.
  - **Radiotherapy:** if high-risk histology or node-positive – external beam ± vaginal brachytherapy.
  - **Chemotherapy:** for advanced/stage III–IV or serous/clear cell – carboplatin + paclitaxel.
  - **Hormonal therapy:** for low-grade, advanced or recurrent (oestrogen receptor positive) – progestogens (medroxyprogesterone acetate [check local policy]) or aromatase inhibitors (if contraindication to progestogen).
  - **Genetic referral:** if personal/family history suggests Lynch syndrome – test for microsatellite instability (MSI) or immunohistochemistry for mismatch repair proteins.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - IV fluids: nil required unless NBM for theatre – then 5% glucose with 0.18% saline or Hartmann’s per local policy.
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40mg SC once daily (unless high bleeding risk or weight <50kg – [check local policy]).
  - Diet: normal diet unless planned for theatre (NBM).
  - Observations: routine (4-hourly) if stable; increase to 1-hourly if bleeding or unstable.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Carboplatin + Paclitaxel** (for advanced disease) – doses per body surface area; follow trust protocol.
  - **Medroxyprogesterone acetate** (hormonal) – typical dose 100–200mg twice daily ([check local policy] — BNF suggests 100–200mg twice daily for advanced breast cancer, used off-label in endometrial cancer).
  - **Tranexamic acid** (for acute heavy bleeding) – 1g IV slowly over 10 minutes, then 1g PO three times daily for up to 5 days.
  - **Enoxaparin** (VTE prophylaxis) – 40mg SC once daily (or 20mg if CrCl <30 or weight <50kg).
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Levonorgestrel intrauterine system (Mirena)** – for low-grade early endometrial cancer in women who wish to preserve fertility (off-label use; under specialist MDT decision).
  - **Megestrol acetate** – alternative progestogen (160mg once daily, [check local policy]).
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Progestogens: avoid in history of thromboembolism (relative) – discuss with gynae oncology.
  - Carboplatin: avoid if CrCl <30 (or dose-reduce per Calvert formula); interacts with nephrotoxic drugs.
  - Paclitaxel: requires premedication (dexamethasone + antihistamine) to prevent hypersensitivity; avoid in severe hepatic impairment.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Carboplatin: dose according to Calvert formula using CrCl – check trust protocol.
  - Paclitaxel: no dose adjustment for mild-moderate renal; avoid if bilirubin >3x ULN.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Carboplatin** – overdosing in impaired renal function can cause severe myelosuppression. Always calculate dose using trust protocol and check CrCl. Never give without confirming the renal function result.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Haemorrhagic shock from heavy vaginal bleeding (0.5–1% of cases)
  - Venous thromboembolism (PE / DVT) – incidence up to 5% around time of diagnosis
  - Ureteric obstruction (advanced disease) – can cause acute kidney injury
  - Bowel obstruction (advanced peritoneal spread)
  - Biopsy-related: uterine perforation (rare), infection, vasovagal reaction
- **Chronic:**
  - Lymphedema after lymphadenectomy (leg swelling)
  - Radiation-induced cystitis / proctitis (if radiotherapy)
  - Hormonal side effects from progestogens (weight gain, depression, thrombosis risk)
  - Recurrence – vaginal vault or distant (lung, liver)
  - Infertility if preservation surgery (hormonal) attempted – need specialist counselling
sections.Prognosis: - Overall 5-year survival ~80% (all stages). **Stage I** (confined to corpus): >90% with surgery. **Stage IV**: <20%. Prognosis worst for serous/clear cell histology and high grade. Recurrence rates ~15% in early stage, much higher in advanced. Most relapses occur within 3 years.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always document bleeding history:** age of menopause, exact pattern of bleeding, tamoxifen use (increased risk), and any personal/family history of Lynch-related cancers (colorectal, ovarian, urothelial).
- **Perform a Pipelle biopsy in clinic before referral** – it can be done with minimal training; check that the patient is not pregnant first.
- **Start VTE prophylaxis on admission** – all gynaecological cancer patients are high risk (use risk assessment tool). Do not wait for diagnosis of cancer.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do NOT discharge a postmenopausal woman with PMB and ET >4mm without a biopsy** – delaying diagnosis worsens stage.
- **Do NOT assume benign atrophy just because ultrasound shows thin endometrium** – a small proportion of cancers have ET <4mm. If bleeding persists, still need biopsy.
- **Do NOT give hormonal therapy (e.g., Mirena) for suspected cancer without histological confirmation** – overtreatment or undertreatment may occur.
- **Do NOT omit pregnancy test in a premenopausal woman with abnormal bleeding** – risk of missing ectopic or early pregnancy.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Any episode of heavy bleeding (soaking through pad in <1 hour)
  - Severe pelvic or lower abdominal pain (especially if accompanied by fever)
  - Sudden shortness of breath or chest pain (suspected PE)
  - New inability to pass urine or stool (ureteric/bowel obstruction)
- **Follow-up:**
  - Urgent gynae outpatient appointment (within 2 weeks per cancer pathway) for biopsy results (if not already done).
  - If inpatient biopsy done, discuss results with gynae oncology within 1 week.
  - Refer to clinical nurse specialist for counselling and pre-operative assessment if surgery planned.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / gynae reg immediately if:**
  - Haemodynamically unstable (systolic BP <90, HR >120) with vaginal bleeding
  - Massive visible bleeding (soaking >1 pad every 15–20 min)
  - Septic picture (fever >38°C, tachycardia, hypotension) post-biopsy
  - Acute severe pelvic pain (suspected perforation)
- **Call crash team if:**
  - Raised NEWS2 (≥7) or falling GCS
  - Oxygen saturations <90% on 15 L O₂ (suspected massive PE)
- **Specific thresholds:**
  - **NEWS2 ≥5** – escalate to ward nurse in charge and review by doctor within 15 minutes.
  - **Lactate >4 mmol/L** – suggests shock, consider critical care referral.
  - **GCS ≤13** – consider hypoglycaemia, sepsis, or acute intracranial event (rare).

rawMarkdown:
## Endometrial Cancer
**One-liner:** Most common gynaecological malignancy in developed countries; typically postmenopausal; presents with abnormal uterine bleeding; usually diagnosed early.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Massive acute vaginal bleed with haemorrhagic shock** – rare but life-threatening. Key finding: **haemodynamic instability** (tachycardia, hypotension, pallor) with visible heavy bleeding. Rule out by assessing pulse, BP, and perineal pad count.
- **Pulmonary embolism (PE) from cancer-associated hypercoagulability** – can present with sudden dyspnoea, pleuritic chest pain, hypoxia. Key finding: **unexplained hypoxia (SpO₂ <94% on air) + tachycardia** with no other obvious cause. Urgent CT pulmonary angiogram if high Wells’ score.
- **Uterine perforation or sepsis following endometrial biopsy** – very rare but consider if severe pelvic pain, fever, or peritonism within hours of procedure. Key finding: **rebound tenderness / guarding** post-biopsy.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Postmenopausal bleeding (PMB) – any bleeding ≥12 months after menopause (the “classic” symptom)
  - Perimenopausal / intermenstrual bleeding (in younger women)
  - Thin, blood-stained discharge (serosanguinous)
  - Pelvic pain or pressure (advanced disease)
  - Abdominal distension (advanced: ascites, mass)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Speculum: visible bleeding from os; atrophic or friable cervix; polypoid mass protruding through cervix
  - Bimanual: enlarged, irregular, or fixed uterus; adnexal mass (if ovarian extension)
  - General: pallor (anaemia), lymphadenopathy (inguinal or supraclavicular), hepatomegaly (metastatic)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Benign endometrial atrophy** – PMB with thin endometrium on ultrasound (<4mm), but still needs biopsy to exclude cancer
- **Endometrial polyp / submucosal fibroid** – sometimes indistinguishable; biopsy/hysteroscopy required
- **Cervical cancer** – visible lesion on speculum, watery/offensive discharge; differentiate by cervix appearance + cytology
- **Vulvar / vaginal cancer** – visible lesion, different site
- **Uterine sarcomas** – faster growth, often larger, may have “fleshy” tissue passed; requires histology
- **Hormone-related anovulatory bleeding (perimenopausal)** – irregular cycles, no PMB; endometrial thickness variable
- **Splitter:** If bleeding is truly postmenopausal (no periods >1 year), **endometrial cancer is the diagnosis until proven otherwise** — do not assume benign atrophy without biopsy.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Full set of observations (pulse, BP, SpO₂, NEWS2)
  - Urine pregnancy test (always, to exclude pregnancy-related causes in premenopausal)
  - Speculum examination + bimanual (as above)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (look for anaemia from chronic blood loss)
  - Renal function / liver function (pre-surgery baseline, also look for obstructive jaundice)
  - Coagulation screen (if heavy bleeding or planned biopsy)
  - Ca125 (elevation suggests advanced stage or serous histology; not diagnostic)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Transvaginal ultrasound (TVUSS) – measures endometrial thickness (ET). Cut-off: >4mm in postmenopausal women (NICE) warrants further assessment. Visualise endometrium: regular vs irregular, myometrial invasion? Adnexal masses?
  - **Second-line:** MRI pelvis (for local staging) – assess depth of myometrial invasion, cervical involvement, lymph nodes
  - CT chest/abdomen/pelvis (if high risk or suspicious of metastases)
- **Special tests:**
  - Endometrial biopsy (Pipelle) in clinic – gold standard for histology. If Pipelle fails or bleeding too heavy, hysteroscopy + targeted biopsy under GA.
  - Histology: look for type (endometrioid vs serous/clear cell) + grade + oestrogen receptor status
- **Diagnostic criteria:** No single scoring system. Diagnosis is histopathological confirmation from biopsy or hysterectomy specimen.
- **Never-miss test:** **Endometrial biopsy** – omission is a serious error. A woman with PMB and ET >4mm must have tissue diagnosis before being discharged. If Pipelle yields insufficient tissue, escalate.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If haemodynamically unstable from heavy bleeding: IV access, cross-match, fluid resuscitation, consider tranexamic acid 1g IV (even though not first-line for cancer bleeding, may reduce loss). Inform gynae reg immediately.
  - If suspected PE: high-flow oxygen, check D-dimer (if low clinical probability) or arrange urgent CT pulmonary angiogram. Start LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 1.5mg/kg once daily) if PE confirmed pending anticoagulation.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Admit under gynae oncology team.
  - Arrange endometrial biopsy if not yet done (inpatient if unable to do as outpatient).
  - Staging MRI pelvis (usually done as outpatient, but if inpatient: order if high suspicion).
  - Start VTE prophylaxis (LMWH) unless bleeding contraindicates – all cancer patients at high risk.
  - Check FBC – arrange transfusion if Hb <80 (or symptomatic).
  - Refer to gynae oncology clinical nurse specialist for support and counselling.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Surgery (standard for early stage):** total hysterectomy + bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy ± pelvic lymphadenectomy. Minimally invasive approach preferred.
  - **Radiotherapy:** if high-risk histology or node-positive – external beam ± vaginal brachytherapy.
  - **Chemotherapy:** for advanced/stage III–IV or serous/clear cell – carboplatin + paclitaxel.
  - **Hormonal therapy:** for low-grade, advanced or recurrent (oestrogen receptor positive) – progestogens (medroxyprogesterone acetate [check local policy]) or aromatase inhibitors (if contraindication to progestogen).
  - **Genetic referral:** if personal/family history suggests Lynch syndrome – test for microsatellite instability (MSI) or immunohistochemistry for mismatch repair proteins.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - IV fluids: nil required unless NBM for theatre – then 5% glucose with 0.18% saline or Hartmann’s per local policy.
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40mg SC once daily (unless high bleeding risk or weight <50kg – [check local policy]).
  - Diet: normal diet unless planned for theatre (NBM).
  - Observations: routine (4-hourly) if stable; increase to 1-hourly if bleeding or unstable.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Carboplatin + Paclitaxel** (for advanced disease) – doses per body surface area; follow trust protocol.
  - **Medroxyprogesterone acetate** (hormonal) – typical dose 100–200mg twice daily ([check local policy] — BNF suggests 100–200mg twice daily for advanced breast cancer, used off-label in endometrial cancer).
  - **Tranexamic acid** (for acute heavy bleeding) – 1g IV slowly over 10 minutes, then 1g PO three times daily for up to 5 days.
  - **Enoxaparin** (VTE prophylaxis) – 40mg SC once daily (or 20mg if CrCl <30 or weight <50kg).
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Levonorgestrel intrauterine system (Mirena)** – for low-grade early endometrial cancer in women who wish to preserve fertility (off-label use; under specialist MDT decision).
  - **Megestrol acetate** – alternative progestogen (160mg once daily, [check local policy]).
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Progestogens: avoid in history of thromboembolism (relative) – discuss with gynae oncology.
  - Carboplatin: avoid if CrCl <30 (or dose-reduce per Calvert formula); interacts with nephrotoxic drugs.
  - Paclitaxel: requires premedication (dexamethasone + antihistamine) to prevent hypersensitivity; avoid in severe hepatic impairment.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Carboplatin: dose according to Calvert formula using CrCl – check trust protocol.
  - Paclitaxel: no dose adjustment for mild-moderate renal; avoid if bilirubin >3x ULN.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Carboplatin** – overdosing in impaired renal function can cause severe myelosuppression. Always calculate dose using trust protocol and check CrCl. Never give without confirming the renal function result.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Haemorrhagic shock from heavy vaginal bleeding (0.5–1% of cases)
  - Venous thromboembolism (PE / DVT) – incidence up to 5% around time of diagnosis
  - Ureteric obstruction (advanced disease) – can cause acute kidney injury
  - Bowel obstruction (advanced peritoneal spread)
  - Biopsy-related: uterine perforation (rare), infection, vasovagal reaction
- **Chronic:**
  - Lymphedema after lymphadenectomy (leg swelling)
  - Radiation-induced cystitis / proctitis (if radiotherapy)
  - Hormonal side effects from progestogens (weight gain, depression, thrombosis risk)
  - Recurrence – vaginal vault or distant (lung, liver)
  - Infertility if preservation surgery (hormonal) attempted – need specialist counselling

### Prognosis
- Overall 5-year survival ~80% (all stages). **Stage I** (confined to corpus): >90% with surgery. **Stage IV**: <20%. Prognosis worst for serous/clear cell histology and high grade. Recurrence rates ~15% in early stage, much higher in advanced. Most relapses occur within 3 years.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always document bleeding history:** age of menopause, exact pattern of bleeding, tamoxifen use (increased risk), and any personal/family history of Lynch-related cancers (colorectal, ovarian, urothelial).
- **Perform a Pipelle biopsy in clinic before referral** – it can be done with minimal training; check that the patient is not pregnant first.
- **Start VTE prophylaxis on admission** – all gynaecological cancer patients are high risk (use risk assessment tool). Do not wait for diagnosis of cancer.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do NOT discharge a postmenopausal woman with PMB and ET >4mm without a biopsy** – delaying diagnosis worsens stage.
- **Do NOT assume benign atrophy just because ultrasound shows thin endometrium** – a small proportion of cancers have ET <4mm. If bleeding persists, still need biopsy.
- **Do NOT give hormonal therapy (e.g., Mirena) for suspected cancer without histological confirmation** – overtreatment or undertreatment may occur.
- **Do NOT omit pregnancy test in a premenopausal woman with abnormal bleeding** – risk of missing ectopic or early pregnancy.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Any episode of heavy bleeding (soaking through pad in <1 hour)
  - Severe pelvic or lower abdominal pain (especially if accompanied by fever)
  - Sudden shortness of breath or chest pain (suspected PE)
  - New inability to pass urine or stool (ureteric/bowel obstruction)
- **Follow-up:**
  - Urgent gynae outpatient appointment (within 2 weeks per cancer pathway) for biopsy results (if not already done).
  - If inpatient biopsy done, discuss results with gynae oncology within 1 week.
  - Refer to clinical nurse specialist for counselling and pre-operative assessment if surgery planned.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / gynae reg immediately if:**
  - Haemodynamically unstable (systolic BP <90, HR >120) with vaginal bleeding
  - Massive visible bleeding (soaking >1 pad every 15–20 min)
  - Septic picture (fever >38°C, tachycardia, hypotension) post-biopsy
  - Acute severe pelvic pain (suspected perforation)
- **Call crash team if:**
  - Raised NEWS2 (≥7) or falling GCS
  - Oxygen saturations <90% on 15 L O₂ (suspected massive PE)
- **Specific thresholds:**
  - **NEWS2 ≥5** – escalate to ward nurse in charge and review by doctor within 15 minutes.
  - **Lactate >4 mmol/L** – suggests shock, consider critical care referral.
  - **GCS ≤13** – consider hypoglycaemia, sepsis, or acute intracranial event (rare).
